title: APW Year 3 AI Report
text: Schools could choose whether or not to use AI in October. The following analysis considers the results of pupils matched between the two sessions.

title: APW Year 3 AI Report
text:
Descriptive Statistics and Score Distributions
The February results of the two groups were very similar. After using AI in October, the mean of the scores was again similar, but the spread narrowed for AI users.

title: APW Year 3 AI Report
text:
Correlations
Regression lines show the average progress of pupils from February to October was similar whether or not their school used AI.

Correlations for AI users were higher than for non-AI users, though both were strong.

title: APW Year 3 AI Report
section: Regression to the Mean and Progress Levels
text:
Regression to the Mean and Progress Levels
AI users had proportionally more pupils with a higher than average score in October making less than average progress. In other words, the regression to the mean effect is more pronounced for AI users.

title: APW Year 3 AI Report
text:
Number of Comparisons & Infit Summary
The number of comparisons completed by AI was substantially higher for AI users than non-AI users. A higher number of comparisons is likely to lead to more reliable measurement. Infit greater than 1.3 is a measure of consistency of the judging. The percentage of pupils with infit over 1.3 was much lower for the group using AI. However, infit is sensitive to the number of comparisons made, so the difference does not necessarily reflect more consistency in the AI judging than the human.

title: APW Year 3 AI Report
text:
Conclusion
Overall, there is little to differentiate the performance of pupils whose schools used AI in October from those that did not. Both groups made similar average progress from February to October, with strong correlations between the two assessments.
The small differences that do appear, in the distributions and in the regression to the mean, may be explained by the higher number of comparisons completed by AI users, which is likely to lead to more reliable measurement.
What the analysis cannot show is whether the AI rewarded different skills or approaches to writing than human judges. Further qualitative analysis of the types of writing rewarded by each method would be needed to explore this question.
